title: Catch-All Domain Synthetic Identity Farm with Incremental Prefix Testing
narrative: Fraudster exploits catch-all mail server (e.g., obscure .tk or .ml domains) to generate unlimited synthetic identities without email validation. Pattern shows 15-40 transactions over 3-7 days using same P_emaildomain but systematically varying prefixes (user001@, user002@, etc.). Each prefix tied to different card1 value, suggesting BIN testing with synthetic accounts. TransactionAmt clusters tightly $45-$89 (below fraud review thresholds). All transactions share identical dist1 (847 miles) and addr1, indicating single shipping drop point. R_emaildomain always null (digital goods, ProductCD=W). Velocity spikes: 8-12 transactions per day from same IP subnet, all during txn_hour 2-6 AM EST. No legitimate business uses catch-all domains with sequential username patterns and distance/address invariance.
indicators: P_emaildomain is catch-all enabled (.tk, .ml, .ga, .cf TLDs), R_emaildomain null across all transactions
15-40 transactions over 3-7 days with identical P_emaildomain but systematically incremented prefixes (user001@, user002@, sequential pattern)
Each unique email prefix paired with distinct card1 value, card1_txn_count_24h = 1 for all cards
TransactionAmt variance <$50 across sequence, mean $45-$89, all ProductCD=W (digital goods)
dist1 identical across all transactions (single constant like 847 miles), addr1 invariant
Temporal clustering: 8-12 transactions daily during txn_hour 2-6 AM, is_night=1
card1_amt_zscore uniformly low (<0.5) due to first-use cards with similar amounts
summary: A single domain with catch-all email (often obscure TLDs like .tk or .ml) generates 15-40 transactions over several days, each using a sequentially numbered email prefix (user001@, user002@) paired with a different card but identical shipping distance and address. All transactions are for digital goods in a tight dollar range ($45-$89) and cluster during early morning hours.
reasoning: The fraudster is mass-creating synthetic identities to test stolen card numbers while staying under review thresholds, using a catch-all domain to bypass email verification and a single drop point to collect digital goods. Sequential prefixes with invariant shipping details reveal coordinated abuse rather than organic customer behavior.
indicator_explanations: Email domain is disposable throwaway service with no billing contact verification
Email addresses follow automated sequential numbering pattern suggesting script-generated accounts
Every new email gets matched with a fresh card number
Purchase amounts stay suspiciously uniform suggesting automated testing of card validity
All transactions originate from identical geographic coordinates despite different cards
Activity clusters in overnight hours when fraud monitoring is lightest
Spending amounts look normal because cards are brand-new with no history
